sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 27, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: KEY INSIGHT - Targets move with the player! When player moved UP 6 rows, all targets moved UP 6 rows. Green target was "collected" (snapped to trail row, shifted left 6 cols). Red target at rows 13-16 (6 rows above trail at 20-21), blue at 19-22 (aligned with trail). Trail at cols 16-46.
- Goal model: Collect all 3 targets. Green collected, blue aligned with trail but not shifted, red not aligned.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves player vertically, RIGHT extends trail. Need to figure out how to "collect" targets.
- Plan: Try extending trail further RIGHT to see if red target snaps, or try other actions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 26 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The RIGHT action only changed 1 cell (total_cells_changed: 1). The trail is still at cols 16-46, and the targets haven't moved. So the trail can't extend further to the right - it's at its maximum length.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The trail is at cols 16-46, rows 20-21. The targets are:
- Red: rows 13-16, cols 42-45
- Blue: rows 19-22, cols 42-45
- Green: rows 19-22, cols 36-39
The green target was collected (shifted left by 6 cols). The blue target is aligned with the trail but hasn't shifted. The red target is not aligned with the trail.
Since the trail can't extend further, maybe I need to try a different approach. Let me think about what ACTION7 does. Earlier, ACTION7 moved the player from rows 36-41 to rows 30-35. Maybe ACTION7 has a different effect now.
Or maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on targets. Let me try clicking on the red target.
Actually, let me reconsider the panel at the bottom. It shows: pink (player), red, green, blue. This might indicate the order in which targets should be collected. The green target has been collected (shifted left). The blue target is aligned with the trail. The red target is not aligned.
Wait, maybe the panel shows the order: first collect red, then green, then blue. But I collected green first. Maybe that's wrong.
Let me try a different approach. Let me try ACTION7 to see what it does now.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Trail at max length (cols 16-46). RIGHT only changed 1 cell (trail can't extend further). Green collected (shifted left), blue aligned with trail, red not aligned. Targets move with player.
Goal model: Need to figure out how to collect red and blue targets. Panel order: red, green, blue.
Plan: Try ACTION7 to see what it does now, or try MOUSE to click on targets.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try ACTION7 to see what it does
result = action(['ACTION7'])
print("Result:", last_action_result)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Check all targets
for color, name in [('R', 'red'), ('b', 'blue'), ('N', 'green')]:
    targets = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == color and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
    for t in targets:
        rows = [p[0] for p in t['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in t['boundary']]
        print(f"{name}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check trail
trail_elements = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] in ['g', 'w'] and 15 <= n['boundary'][0][1] <= 50 and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
if trail_elements:
    max_col = max([max(p[1] for p in t['boundary']) for t in trail_elements])
    min_col = min([min(p[1] for p in t['boundary']) for t in trail_elements])
    trail_rows = set()
    for t in trail_elements:
        for p in t['boundary']:
            trail_rows.add(p[0])
    print(f"Trail: cols {min_col}-{max_col}, rows: {sorted(trail_rows)}")

# Check player
player = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18][0]
print(f"Player: boundary={player['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
